lessonTitle: Fertility and Contraception in Sickle Cell Disease

data.title: Rate your current level before this lesson

questions.question: My knowledge about Fertility, Contraception, and Pregnancy in SCD on a scale of 1 to 10 is ___ .
My comfort level with Fertility, Contraception, and Pregnancy in SCD on a scale of 1 to 10 is ___ .
My confidence level with Fertility, Contraception, and Pregnancy for people with SCD on a scale of 1 to 10 is ___ .

items.html: Discuss contraceptive options for women with sickle cell disease (SCD)
Discuss strategies to optimize the chances of conception
Review pregnancy risks for women with SCD
Review options for reducing the chances of having a child with SCD

items.title: What Is Infertility?
items.html: Infertility is defined as the failure to achieve a pregnancy after at least 12 months of regular unprotected intercourse. Failure to conceive despite regular intercourse, irregular menstrual cycles > 35 days apart, or difficulty in achieving regular intercourse due to coital dysfunction are reasons to seek medical advice.

items.title: Fertile Window
items.html: 6 most fertile days finishing on the day of ovulation. Patients should be advised to have frequent or well timed intercourse at least 2-3 days during the fertile window.

items.title: Ovulation
items.html: Ovulation can be estimated by subtracting 14 days from the average menstrual cycle length.

items.title: Smartphone App
items.html: You can use a fertility application on a smartphone or over-the-counter ovulation predictor kits that detect urinary LH levels to help time intercourse appropriately during the fertile window.

data.html: Individuals with sickle cell disease have similar causes and risk factors for infertility as the general population.

items.html: Ovulatory Dysfunction
Tubal Disease
Uterocervical Factors
Male Factor
Unexplained Infertility

slides.title: Factors Impacting Fertility in Individuals with SCD

items.title: Hydroxyurea
items.html: Exposure to hydroxyurea may lead to diminished ovarian reserve, while there are significant benefits to hydroxyurea that warrant individualized discussion regarding its use.

items.title: Ovarian Reserve
items.html: It has been suggested that women with SCD have a lower and accelerated decline in ovarian reserve.

items.title: Uterine Fibroids
items.html: African individuals have a high prevalence of uterine fibroids which may impact fertility.

items.title: Males with SCD
items.html:
There are a lack of data around fertility for women, while men with SCD have been shown to have infertility due to:
  • Hypogonadism
  • Sperm abnormalities
  • Erectile dysfunction (ED) due to priapism

data.html: To reduce sickle cell births, understanding inheritance is of paramount importance for counselling your patient.

images.caption: Understanding Inheritance in SCD. Photo credit: https://sicklecellanemiahonorsbio.weebly.com/source-links.html

slides.title: Reducing the Number of SCD Births

data.html:
Screen the partner for sickle cell trait with a CBC and hemoglobin electrophoresis. If the partner has a hemoglobinopathy, then referral to a fertility clinic is recommended to review options, including in vitro fertilization with preimplantation genetic testing for having a child. (More to come on this later). There are options for antenatal testing, each with their own risks and benefits, and include:

  • Chorionic Villus Sampling at 11-14 weeks
  • Amniocentesis at >15 weeks

slides.title: Risks of Having a Child with SCD

items.html: Deciding how, when, and if to have children can be an emotionally challenging, particularly when there is a chance of having a child with SCD.
Stigma surrounding reproductive choices, including pursing fertility treatments, abortion, or having an offspring with SCD adds to the complexity.
Financial barriers may further impact decision making.

data.question: Which of the following are fertility concerns for individuals pregnant with Sickle Cell Disease? (Select all that apply)
data.correct_answer: Ovulatory Dysfunction; Tubal Disease; Uterocervical Factors; Unexplained infertility
data.feedback_correct: Individuals with sickle cell disease have similar causes and risk factors for infertility as the general population, including Ovulatory Dysfunction, Tubal Disease, Uterocervical Factors, Male Factor, and Unexplained Infertility.

items.html: Let's look at the options for conception.
Natural conception with option for testing of SCD in pregnancy (amniocentesis/chorionic villus sampling) or after birth (newborn screening/electrophoresis).
Donor gametes (donor sperm or donor egg from donor with no hemoglobinopathy). Child will have sickle cell trait (heterozygous carrier state - Hb AS).

Gestational carrier: Sperm & egg provided by intended parents or donors, with conception via IVF and embryo transfer to gestational carrier. This is beneficial for women with significant health issues for which pregnancy is considered high risk.
Adopting a child who is free of sickle cell disease.
Assisted Reproductive Technologies: In vitro fertilization to develop embryo and pre implantation testing of embryos for presence of sickle cell (IVF with PGT-M).
Fertility Preservation: Through oocyte or ovarian tissue cryopreservation, for people whose future fertility is at risk, such as seen after stem cell transplant.

images.caption: Types of PGT — For SCD, PGT-M is used

before.heading: PGT-M
before.text: PGT-M is for monogenic disorders which are disorders that are affected by a single gene and includes SCD

after.heading: PGT-M Technology
after.text: PGT-M technology allows for the identification of affected embryos so that only the unaffected embryos may be transferred into the uterus

slides.title: IVF Considerations for People with SCD

items.html: Tailored medications to reduce risk of ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS)
Multidisciplinary team approach
Supportive counseling and encouragement.
High live birth rates have been published for couples doing IVF with PGT-M

slides.title: Contraception and Pre Conception In Sickle Cell Disease

data.html:
To aid in informed decision making for individuals with SCD, we will briefly review contraception and pre-conception.

Although there are limited data on the reproductive health for individuals with SCD, there are clear suggestions of:

items.html: higher rates of unintended pregnancies
with lower than average use of contraception

data.html:
Among other factors, decisions about contraception should be encouraged.

Contraception is the prevention of pregnancy and examples include:

items.html: barrier methods
intrauterine devices
progesterone-only hormonal contraception (progestin only pill, Depot progestin or progestin-secreting intrauterine system) which has been shown to potentially lower vaso-occlusive episodes (VOE)

data.html:
Oral contraceptive pill, patch, or ring – contraindicated in women with a history of venous thromboembolism

For those trying to prevent pregnancy, we must facilitate access to safe and effective contraception.

For those who would like to conceive a child, they must be provided with appropriate pre-conception screening and genetic counseling for individuals with SCD and their partners.
PGT-M is for monogenic disorders which are disorders that are affected by a single gene and includes Sickle Cell Disease.

items.html: People with SCD can experience infertility due to the same causes at the general population but may be more likely to have diminished ovarian reserve
Several options exist for achieving pregnancy and people at risk of having a child with SCD should be counselled about options including IVF with PGT-M
Fertility preservation is an important consideration in people facing a potential stem cell transplant
People with SCD are at high risk for unintended pregnancy and should be counselled about contraceptive options
